title: The Optimal Error Resilience of Interactive Communication over the Binary Alphabet
speaker: Rachel Zhang
abstract: In interactive coding, Alice and Bob wish to compute some function f of their private inputs x and y. They do this by engaging in a non-adaptive (fixed speaking order, fixed length) protocol to jointly compute f(x,y). The goal is to do this in an error-resilient way, such that even if some fraction of the communication is adversarially corrupted, both parties still learn f(x,y).

proof_stages.label: Binary error-correcting-code baseline
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up ordinary error-correcting codes, adversarial errors and erasures, relative distance and resilience, and the asymptotic binary limitation of distance one-half together with the crucial finite-message exception.

proof_stages.label: Interactive coding model
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines the task of compiling a noiseless two-party protocol into a fixed-order noisy protocol whose transcript can still be recovered under a bounded fraction of adversarial corruptions.

proof_stages.label: Known bounds and optimal target theorem
proof_stages.summary: Prior large-alphabet and binary results are compared, the new theorem is stated as achieving the matching binary optima of one-sixth error resilience and one-half erasure resilience with positive rate and efficiency, and the feedback model is identified as the source of the one-sixth target.

proof_stages.label: Message-exchange reduction and one-eighth baseline
proof_stages.summary: For exposition the protocol is reduced to exchanging two private messages, where directly sending binary ECCs gives only one-eighth error resilience, so the proof must communicate in short updates that can use two-thirds-distance encodings.

proof_stages.label: One-sided question-answer synchronization
proof_stages.summary: In the simplified one-sided setting Bob repeatedly sends his current guess and Alice answers with the next bit or a backspace, with the answer encoded among three two-thirds-separated options and then echoed with the received question to avoid updating on corrupted questions.

proof_stages.label: Two-sided protocol and asymmetric distance code
proof_stages.summary: Both parties now maintain guesses and send question-plus-answer messages, using a special binary code whose three possible answers at a fixed pair of questions have distance two-thirds while other pairs retain distance one-half, because enforcing two-thirds everywhere would make questions too fragile.

proof_stages.label: Randomized update rule for half-distance corruptions
proof_stages.summary: Since a half-distance corruption can change both the answer and the associated question, the protocol updates with probability one on a matching question but only probability one-half on a mismatched question, restoring positive expected progress toward the correct guesses.

proof_stages.label: Lifting to efficient full interactive coding
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains that positive rate is obtained by tree-code-like compression, computational efficiency by chunking and hashing techniques, and full interactive protocols by tracking transcript guesses rather than just exchanged messages, leaving derandomization as an open problem.

examples.name: Uncoded two-bit message corrupted from 01 to 11
examples.note: The toy corruption makes concrete why Bob cannot infer Alice's intended message from a noisy uncoded transmission, motivating ordinary error-correcting codes.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker traces the specific corruption 01→11 and checks the resulting ambiguity between possible original messages

passes.reason: A routine uncoded two-bit transmission is used to show that a single bit flip can make decoding ambiguous.
An ordinary two-bit transmission is used to show that one bit flip makes uncoded decoding ambiguous.
an ordinary raw two-bit transmission is used to show uncoded sending can be defeated by one bit flip
The uncoded identity transmission has no redundancy and is used to show raw sending can become ambiguous after one bit flip.
the trivial no-redundancy transmission is used to show raw sending fails under a single bit flip
the identity/no-redundancy two-bit transmission is used to show a single flip makes raw communication ambiguous
A trivial uncoded transmission is used as the adversarial one-bit-flip ambiguity showing raw sending is not error-resilient.
An uncoded identity transmission has no redundancy, and a one-bit adversarial flip makes decoding ambiguous.
A single-bit flip of an uncoded transmission is used to show direct sending is not error-resilient.
a single flip in the uncoded transmission makes the received word ambiguous, showing uncoded communication is not error-resilient
the identity/no-redundancy two-bit transmission is defeated by a single bit flip
the trivial uncoded two-bit transmission is used to show that a single flip can make decoding ambiguous

examples.name: Four-word binary code of relative distance two-thirds
examples.note: The small binary code for four possible two-bit messages is presented as the observation that reveals why constant-size answers can beat the asymptotic one-half binary distance barrier.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker examines the four binary codewords, verifies their pairwise relative distance is two-thirds, and derives the resulting error/erasure resilience

passes.reason: The four-word equidistant binary code attains the optimal finite-size distance two-thirds and is used to defeat the naive finite reading of the binary one-half bound.
The equidistant four-word binary code is optimal for that tiny parameter range and refutes the finite-code reading of the binary one-half distance bound.
the four-word length-three binary simplex code is an equidistant optimal small code and illustrates the failure of the unqualified one-half distance slogan
The four codewords form the optimal equidistant binary simplex code of relative distance 2/3, presented as a positive small-code ingredient.
the equidistant four-word binary code is a finite optimal-looking/simplex code used to refute the naive finite reading of the one-half bound
the four codewords form an equidistant binary [3,2,2] simplex/parity code, optimal for that size, and rebut the unqualified one-half-distance intuition for finite codes
The four codewords form an optimal equidistant binary simplex/tetrahedron code used as a finite high-distance building block.
The four-word length-3 binary code is an optimal equidistant code used to refute the finite-code reading of the binary distance-one-half bound.
The equidistant four-word binary code is used to refute a literal finite-size reading of the binary distance-1/2 bound and attains the optimal small-code distance 2/3.
the four binary codewords are equidistant at relative distance 2/3, giving a symmetric finite code meeting the small-code optimum
the four length-3 codewords form an equidistant tetrahedral (3,4,2) code attaining the finite optimum while exposing the finite-size caveat to the 1/2 bound
the equidistant four-word binary simplex code attains the optimal finite distance 2/3 and shows the 1/2 binary bound is only asymptotic

examples.name: Degenerate all-zero/all-one binary code
examples.note: The audience-raised two-codeword construction illustrates why the binary distance upper bound must be interpreted for large code families with meaningful rate or message size.
examples.engagement_reason: the all-zero/all-one code is only cited as a degenerate high-distance caveat, without any construction or calculation beyond stating the fact

passes.reason: The two-codeword repetition code is a trivial maximally distant code, cited to show why large code size/rate assumptions are needed.
The two-word repetition code is collapsed to all-zero/all-one codewords, has maximal relative distance one, and is cited to show size/rate assumptions are necessary.
the repetition code {0^n,1^n} is a maximally separated minimal code used to show high distance is possible without large code size assumptions
The all-zero/all-one repetition code is a trivial two-word code attaining maximum distance 1 and is invoked to defeat unqualified distance claims without a largeness/rate assumption.
the two constant antipodal codewords form a zero-rate trivial code with maximal distance, illustrating why size/rate assumptions are needed
the two antipodal constant codewords form a trivial repetition code with maximal distance one, raised as a caveat to distance claims without large-code or rate assumptions
The two constant codewords are a trivial repetition code with maximal relative distance 1, invoked to expose missing large-code or rate assumptions.
The all-zero/all-one repetition code is a two-codeword degenerate antipodal code with maximal relative distance, used to show the need for code-size/rate assumptions.
The two-codeword repetition code shows that without a large-code/rate assumption one can get distance 1, the maximum possible, but only in a trivial low-rate case.
the repetition code {0^n,1^n} has maximal distance 1 but only a trivial two-word low-rate structure, exposing missing size/rate assumptions
the two-word repetition code {0^n,1^n} is a trivial maximally distant and highly symmetric code used to defeat bounds without a size/rate condition
the two-codeword all-zero/all-one repetition code is a trivial antipodal maximum-distance code used to defeat any bound lacking a large-code assumption

examples.name: Chess as an interactive protocol
examples.note: Encoding each chess move separately fails after one adversarially corrupted move, showing why interactive coding needs synchronization, error detection, and rollback rather than ordinary block coding alone.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker runs the encoded-move idea on chess and reasons through how corrupting the first move makes Alice and Bob maintain different game states

passes.reason: Chess is an ordinary motivating interactive protocol rather than a structurally special object or the counterexample itself.
Chess is a representative alternating protocol used to show that independently encoding each move can fail after one adversarial corruption.
chess is an ordinary interactive protocol used as the setting where one corrupted move defeats naive per-message encoding
Chess is an ordinary interactive protocol used to illustrate how per-message coding can be broken by an adversarial first-move corruption.
ordinary chess is used as an interactive protocol where one early corrupted move defeats the naive encode-each-message idea
chess is an ordinary alternating protocol used with a first-move corruption to show per-message ECC does not suffice for interactive communication
Chess is an ordinary interactive protocol used to show that one corrupted move defeats naive per-message encoding.
Chess is an ordinary turn-taking protocol used to show that independently encoding each move can desynchronize after one corrupted move.
Chess is used as a familiar representative interactive protocol, not as a structurally special object.
the chess protocol supplies the enemy case where one corrupted first move makes a naively encoded interactive simulation diverge
chess is an ordinary interactive protocol used as an enemy case where one corrupted first move makes local encoding fail
chess is an ordinary interactive protocol used to demonstrate that per-message encoding can be desynchronized by one adversarially corrupted move

examples.name: Repeat-until-received bit over an erasure channel
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker explains the erasure intuition by imagining one party repeatedly sending a bit while the other reports whether it was received.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker concretely describes the repeat-until-received interaction, with one party resending a bit until the other acknowledges receiving it

passes.reason: The repeat-until-received bit protocol is a standard positive illustration of how interaction handles erasures.
The repeat-until-received gadget is a minimal one-bit erasure-channel base case used as positive intuition rather than as a failure case.
the stop-and-wait transmission of a single bit is a minimal erasure-protocol gadget used constructively
The repeat-until-received bit is a standard ARQ-style erasure protocol used as positive intuition rather than as an obstruction.
the stop-and-wait retransmission subroutine is a standard positive illustration of erasure handling rather than a failure case
the repeat-and-acknowledge scheme is a minimal one-bit erasure-channel protocol illustrating the waiting idea
The repeat-until-received bit protocol is a standard constructive erasure-channel illustration of waiting for information.
This is a minimal one-bit repeat/acknowledge erasure protocol illustrating how interaction can wait out erasures, not a refutation.
The repeat-until-acknowledged one-bit exchange is a minimal erasure-handling subroutine illustrating the construction intuition.
the repeat-until-acknowledged single bit is a minimal stop-and-wait erasure-channel toy illustrating waiting, not a refutation
the one-bit repeat-until-acknowledged routine is a minimal base-case protocol illustrating a positive erasure strategy
the repeat-until-received scheme is a minimal one-bit erasure-channel primitive illustrating how interaction lets parties wait for information

examples.name: Fifteen-bit termination edge case
examples.note: The audience's 15-bit-message scenario clarifies that Bob does not terminate early but keeps updating until the predetermined end and then reads the required prefix.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker analyzes the 15-bit termination edge case, explaining that Bob keeps updating until the fixed end and then takes the first 15 bits

passes.reason: The last-bit fifteen-bit scenario is a boundary termination case used as a possible failure of premature stopping.

the last-bit length-fifteen scenario is a boundary termination case raised as a possible flaw in early stopping
The last-bit termination scenario is a boundary case raised as a possible failure of stopping as soon as a 15-bit guess is formed.
the last-bit termination scenario is a boundary case raised as an obstruction to premature stopping
the last-bit stopping scenario is a boundary case used to expose a possible flaw in early termination
The fifteenth-bit case is a terminal boundary case raised as a possible obstruction to early termination.
The corrupted final bit is a termination-boundary case used to show that stopping as soon as a 15-bit guess appears would be unsafe.
The last-bit/termination scenario is an endpoint case used to expose the failure of naive early stopping.
the corrupted final-bit situation is a boundary termination case showing that stopping as soon as 15 bits are held could fail

examples.name: Corrupting Bob back to the empty guess
examples.note: The worked run where Alice knows Bob's input and Eve corrupts a response to a wrong-question backspace exhibits the obstruction that motivates updating with probability one-half on mismatched questions.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker steps through Bob’s empty guess being extended to 0, then corrupted via a rewind/backspace update back to the empty string, and discusses the probabilistic fix

passes.reason: The adversarial corruption returns Bob to the empty minimal guess, showing the proposed deterministic update rule can make no progress.
The adversary drives Bob back to the empty guess, a collapsed transcript state, to show the naive update rule can make no progress.
the adversarial half-distance corruption returns Bob to the empty guess, a minimal state and tight obstruction to the naive update rule
The adversarial update returns Bob to the empty-string initial guess, showing the naive progress argument can fail.
the attack returns Bob to the empty initial guess, showing the naive deterministic update rule can make no progress
returning Bob to the empty string is a collapsed minimal state and the attack shows the naïve deterministic update can make zero progress
The attack returns Bob to the minimal empty-string guess, showing the proposed update rule can make no progress.
The attack uses Bob’s minimal empty-guess state and a tight half-distance corruption to show deterministic always-update behavior can make no progress.
The adversarial update sends Bob back to the empty guess, a minimal collapsed state, to show the always-update scheme can make no progress.
the adversary uses a half-distance corruption to return Bob to the minimal empty guess, obstructing the always-update progress argument
